mainmatter.p: Let A be a type; an A-algebra X is defined to be a type { \mathopen {} \left \lvert X \right \rvert \mathclose {}} equipped with a function { A } \xrightarrow {{ \alpha _A }}{ { \mathopen {} \left \lvert X \right \rvert \mathclose {}} }. A homomorphism { X } \xrightarrow {{ f }}{ Y } of A-algebras is defined to be a function { { \mathopen {} \left \lvert X \right \rvert \mathclose {}} } \xrightarrow {{ { \mathopen {} \left \lvert f \right \rvert \mathclose {}} }}{ { \mathopen {} \left \lvert Y \right \rvert \mathclose {}} } together with a homotopy \alpha _f \colon { \mathopen {} \left \lvert f \right \rvert \mathclose {}} \circ \alpha _X \sim \alpha _Y. A homotopy H \colon f \sim g between homomorphisms { X } \xrightarrow {{ f,g }}{ Y } is given by a homotopy { \mathopen {} \left \lvert H \right \rvert \mathclose {}} \colon { \mathopen {} \left \lvert f \right \rvert \mathclose {}} \sim { \mathopen {} \left \lvert g \right \rvert \mathclose {}} together with an assignment to each a:A of an identification \alpha _H a \colon { \mathopen {} \left \lvert H \right \rvert \mathclose {}} \alpha _X a \cdot \eta _g a = \eta _f a.

One of the most common tasks when working in univalent foundations is to characterize the path space of a given type, i.e. establish a structure identity principle. Formalized SIP proofs often follow a baroque style involving identity systems and the even more complex displayed identity systems, etc. that sometimes obscure the very simple informal reasoning behind a given structure identity principle; the proposal is to develop a formal proof language to better reflect this informal reasoning, backed by a suite of lemmas and tactics.
To understand what I mean, let us consider how we might characterize the path spaces of morphisms in a co-slice A \downarrow \mathcal {U}.

Let A be a type; an A-algebra X is defined to be a type { \mathopen {} \left \lvert X \right \rvert \mathclose {}} equipped with a function { A } \xrightarrow {{ \alpha _A }}{ { \mathopen {} \left \lvert X \right \rvert \mathclose {}} }. A homomorphism { X } \xrightarrow {{ f }}{ Y } of A-algebras is defined to be a function { { \mathopen {} \left \lvert X \right \rvert \mathclose {}} } \xrightarrow {{ { \mathopen {} \left \lvert f \right \rvert \mathclose {}} }}{ { \mathopen {} \left \lvert Y \right \rvert \mathclose {}} } together with a homotopy \alpha _f \colon { \mathopen {} \left \lvert f \right \rvert \mathclose {}} \circ \alpha _X \sim \alpha _Y. A homotopy H \colon f \sim g between homomorphisms { X } \xrightarrow {{ f,g }}{ Y } is given by a homotopy { \mathopen {} \left \lvert H \right \rvert \mathclose {}} \colon { \mathopen {} \left \lvert f \right \rvert \mathclose {}} \sim { \mathopen {} \left \lvert g \right \rvert \mathclose {}} together with an assignment to each a:A of an identification \alpha _H a \colon { \mathopen {} \left \lvert H \right \rvert \mathclose {}} \alpha _X a \cdot \eta _g a = \eta _f a.

frontmatter.title: Structure identity principle for co-slice morphisms

mainmatter.p: Let A be a type and let { X } \xrightarrow {{ f }}{ Y } be a homomorphism of A-algebras. Assuming function extensionality, the type \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( g \colon X \to Y \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( g \colon X \to Y \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( g \colon X \to Y \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( g \colon X \to Y \right ) \mathclose {}} } } g \sim f of homomorphisms homotopic to f is contractible.

We expand the dependent sum \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( g \colon X \to Y \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( g \colon X \to Y \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( g \colon X \to Y \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( g \colon X \to Y \right ) \mathclose {}} } } g \sim f as a flat record telescope:
mainmatter.tex: \begin {bmatrix*}[l] { \mathopen {} \left \lvert g \right \rvert \mathclose {}} & \colon & { \mathopen {} \left \lvert X \right \rvert \mathclose {}} \to { \mathopen {} \left \lvert Y \right \rvert \mathclose {}} \\ \alpha _g & \colon & { \mathopen {} \left \lvert g \right \rvert \mathclose {}} \circ \alpha _X \sim \alpha _Y \\ { \mathopen {} \left \lvert H \right \rvert \mathclose {}} & \colon & { \mathopen {} \left \lvert f \right \rvert \mathclose {}} \sim { \mathopen {} \left \lvert g \right \rvert \mathclose {}} \\ \alpha _H & \colon & \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( a:A \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( a:A \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( a:A \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( a:A \right ) \mathclose {}} } } { \mathopen {} \left \lvert H \right \rvert \mathclose {}} \alpha _X a \cdot \alpha _g a = \alpha _f a \end {bmatrix*}

\begin {bmatrix*}[l] { \mathopen {} \left \lvert g \right \rvert \mathclose {}} & \colon & { \mathopen {} \left \lvert X \right \rvert \mathclose {}} \to { \mathopen {} \left \lvert Y \right \rvert \mathclose {}} \\ { \mathopen {} \left \lvert H \right \rvert \mathclose {}} & \colon & { \mathopen {} \left \lvert f \right \rvert \mathclose {}} \sim { \mathopen {} \left \lvert g \right \rvert \mathclose {}} \\ \alpha _g & \colon & { \mathopen {} \left \lvert g \right \rvert \mathclose {}} \circ \alpha _X \sim \alpha _Y \\ \alpha _H & \colon & \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( a:A \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( a:A \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( a:A \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( a:A \right ) \mathclose {}} } } { \mathopen {} \left \lvert H \right \rvert \mathclose {}} \alpha _X a \cdot \alpha _g a = \alpha _f a \end {bmatrix*}
mainmatter.p: By homotopy induction (function extensionality), the first two fields contract to a point with substitution { \mathopen {} \left [ { \mathopen {} \left \lvert g \right \rvert \mathclose {}} : \equiv { \mathopen {} \left \lvert f \right \rvert \mathclose {}} ; { \mathopen {} \left \lvert H \right \rvert \mathclose {}} : \equiv \lambda \_ . \mathsf { refl } \right ] \mathclose {}}. What remains of our telescope is the following:
mainmatter.tex: \begin {bmatrix*}[l] \alpha _g & \colon & { \mathopen {} \left \lvert f \right \rvert \mathclose {}} \circ \alpha _X \sim \alpha _Y \\ \alpha _H & \colon & \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( a:A \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( a:A \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( a:A \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( a:A \right ) \mathclose {}} } } \mathsf { refl } \cdot \alpha _g a = \alpha _f a \end {bmatrix*}
mainmatter.p: Zooming into the \alpha _H component, we simplify it and then zoom back out:
mainmatter.tex: \begin {bmatrix*}[l] \alpha _g & \colon & { \mathopen {} \left \lvert f \right \rvert \mathclose {}} \circ \alpha _X \sim \alpha _Y \\ \alpha _H & \colon & \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( a:A \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( a:A \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( a:A \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( a:A \right ) \mathclose {}} } } \alpha _g a = \alpha _f a \end {bmatrix*}
mainmatter.p: By dependent homotopy induction (also function extensionality), the remaining data collapse to a point with substitution { \mathopen {} \left [ \alpha _g : \equiv \alpha _f; \alpha _H : \equiv \lambda \_ . \mathsf { refl } \right ] \mathclose {}}. The remaining telescope is empty, so we are done.

The steps performed in the proof of the SIP lemma for co-slice morphisms above are most naturally expressed at the level of a flat telescope (a context). The goal is always to contract a telescope to a point, and each step is either a rearrangement of the telescope, a “zooming in” step that adjusts a component of the telescope, or an application of some form of identity induction to kill off part of the telescope — path induction, homotopy induction, equivalence induction, etc. In general, we want to be able to easily point out the fragment of a telescope that contracts to a point, and then use the resulting substitution to modify the telescope and proceed further until the entire telescope is gone.

I believe that there is a reasonable and practical formal logic for doing such things, and that this logic can be implemented as a series of lemmas in homotopy type theory that can then be animated via tactics for interactive backward-inference proofs of contractibility.

frontmatter.title: Structure identity principle for co-slice morphisms

mainmatter.p: Let A be a type and let { X } \xrightarrow {{ f }}{ Y } be a homomorphism of A-algebras. Assuming function extensionality, the type \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( g \colon X \to Y \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( g \colon X \to Y \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( g \colon X \to Y \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( g \colon X \to Y \right ) \mathclose {}} } } g \sim f of homomorphisms homotopic to f is contractible.

We expand the dependent sum \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( g \colon X \to Y \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( g \colon X \to Y \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( g \colon X \to Y \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( g \colon X \to Y \right ) \mathclose {}} } } g \sim f as a flat record telescope:
mainmatter.tex: \begin {bmatrix*}[l] { \mathopen {} \left \lvert g \right \rvert \mathclose {}} & \colon & { \mathopen {} \left \lvert X \right \rvert \mathclose {}} \to { \mathopen {} \left \lvert Y \right \rvert \mathclose {}} \\ \alpha _g & \colon & { \mathopen {} \left \lvert g \right \rvert \mathclose {}} \circ \alpha _X \sim \alpha _Y \\ { \mathopen {} \left \lvert H \right \rvert \mathclose {}} & \colon & { \mathopen {} \left \lvert f \right \rvert \mathclose {}} \sim { \mathopen {} \left \lvert g \right \rvert \mathclose {}} \\ \alpha _H & \colon & \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( a:A \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( a:A \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( a:A \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( a:A \right ) \mathclose {}} } } { \mathopen {} \left \lvert H \right \rvert \mathclose {}} \alpha _X a \cdot \alpha _g a = \alpha _f a \end {bmatrix*}

\begin {bmatrix*}[l] { \mathopen {} \left \lvert g \right \rvert \mathclose {}} & \colon & { \mathopen {} \left \lvert X \right \rvert \mathclose {}} \to { \mathopen {} \left \lvert Y \right \rvert \mathclose {}} \\ { \mathopen {} \left \lvert H \right \rvert \mathclose {}} & \colon & { \mathopen {} \left \lvert f \right \rvert \mathclose {}} \sim { \mathopen {} \left \lvert g \right \rvert \mathclose {}} \\ \alpha _g & \colon & { \mathopen {} \left \lvert g \right \rvert \mathclose {}} \circ \alpha _X \sim \alpha _Y \\ \alpha _H & \colon & \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( a:A \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( a:A \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( a:A \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( a:A \right ) \mathclose {}} } } { \mathopen {} \left \lvert H \right \rvert \mathclose {}} \alpha _X a \cdot \alpha _g a = \alpha _f a \end {bmatrix*}
mainmatter.p: By homotopy induction (function extensionality), the first two fields contract to a point with substitution { \mathopen {} \left [ { \mathopen {} \left \lvert g \right \rvert \mathclose {}} : \equiv { \mathopen {} \left \lvert f \right \rvert \mathclose {}} ; { \mathopen {} \left \lvert H \right \rvert \mathclose {}} : \equiv \lambda \_ . \mathsf { refl } \right ] \mathclose {}}. What remains of our telescope is the following:
mainmatter.tex: \begin {bmatrix*}[l] \alpha _g & \colon & { \mathopen {} \left \lvert f \right \rvert \mathclose {}} \circ \alpha _X \sim \alpha _Y \\ \alpha _H & \colon & \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( a:A \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( a:A \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( a:A \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( a:A \right ) \mathclose {}} } } \mathsf { refl } \cdot \alpha _g a = \alpha _f a \end {bmatrix*}
mainmatter.p: Zooming into the \alpha _H component, we simplify it and then zoom back out:
mainmatter.tex: \begin {bmatrix*}[l] \alpha _g & \colon & { \mathopen {} \left \lvert f \right \rvert \mathclose {}} \circ \alpha _X \sim \alpha _Y \\ \alpha _H & \colon & \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( a:A \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( a:A \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( a:A \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( a:A \right ) \mathclose {}} } } \alpha _g a = \alpha _f a \end {bmatrix*}
mainmatter.p: By dependent homotopy induction (also function extensionality), the remaining data collapse to a point with substitution { \mathopen {} \left [ \alpha _g : \equiv \alpha _f; \alpha _H : \equiv \lambda \_ . \mathsf { refl } \right ] \mathclose {}}. The remaining telescope is empty, so we are done.
